bibliographic_data.title: Achieving Linear Speedup with Partial Worker Participation in Non-IID Federated Learning
bibliographic_data.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a distributed machine learning architecture that leverages a large number of workers to jointly learn a model with decentralized data. Existing studies on linear speedup for convergence are limited to i.i.d. datasets and full worker participation, which rarely hold in practice. This paper shows that the federated averaging (FedAvg) algorithm with two-sided learning rates achieves a linear speedup for convergence under non-i.i.d. datasets and partial worker participation.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper demonstrates that the federated averaging (FedAvg) algorithm with two-sided learning rates can achieve linear speedup for convergence under non-i.i.d. datasets and partial worker participation.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use a generalized FedAvg algorithm with two-sided learning rates to analyze the convergence rate in both full and partial worker participation scenarios.
outcomes: Achieved a convergence rate of O(1/√mKT + 1/T) for full worker participation
Achieved a convergence rate of O(√K/√nT + 1/T) for partial worker participation
core_content.full_text_markdown:
Federated learning (FL) is a distributed machine learning paradigm that leverages a large number of workers to collaboratively learn a model with decentralized data under the coordination of a centralized server. The goal of FL is to solve an optimization problem, which can be decomposed as: min_x∈R^d f(x) := 1/m ∑_{i=1}^{m} F_i(x), where F_i(x) ≜ E_ξ_i∼D_i[F_i(x, ξ_i)] is the local (non-convex) loss function associated with a local data distribution D_i and m is the number of workers. FL allows a large number of workers to participate flexibly without sharing data, which helps protect data privacy but introduces unique challenges such as non-i.i.d. datasets across workers and time-varying partial worker participation.

In this paper, the authors show that the federated averaging (FedAvg) algorithm with two-sided learning rates can achieve linear speedup for convergence under non-i.i.d. datasets and partial worker participation. The key outcomes include a convergence rate of O(1/√mKT + 1/T) for full worker participation and O(√K/√nT + 1/T) for partial worker participation, where K is the number of local steps, T is the total communication rounds, m is the total worker number, and n is the worker number in one communication round if for partial worker participation. The results also reveal that local steps can help convergence and suggest a maximum number of local steps can be improved to T/m in full worker participation.
